skill.name: code-context-finder
skill.description: Find relevant coding context by combining memory graph lookups with local code relationship analysis for imports, callers, tests, and prior decisions.
skill.summary: Surfaces related code, tests, and memory context before edits.
skill.author: Joseph OBrien

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, weak-crypto, and sensitive-file patterns, but most are markdown examples or search strings rather than executable behavior. The confirmed risks are legitimate local file traversal and suggested shell searches that can expose sensitive project context, so publication is acceptable with a medium warning.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local File Inspection Can Surface Sensitive Context
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally guides agents to inspect configuration files, environment-file references, and project files. This supports code context discovery, but it can expose secrets or sensitive deployment details to the AI session if used on unrestricted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly target config and .env-style files, and the helper recursively reads files under the selected root. This is a real privacy risk, but it appears tied to legitimate local analysis rather than exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Agent-Run Shell Search Commands Require User Trust
medium_findings.description: The documentation recommends grep, find, and xargs searches across the working tree. The examples are normal code-search operations, but they can read large amounts of local source and should be limited to trusted project directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are documentation examples with placeholder search terms, not hidden execution. The confidence is high enough to warn because the workflow still asks an agent to run broad local searches.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Code-Execution Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Several high and medium static alerts appear to match markdown examples, memory search names, and formatted output text rather than executable weak cryptography or dynamic code execution. No evidence found of cryptographic implementation or eval-style behavior in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown guidance or output formatting, and the Python helper only parses files with pathlib and regex. I did not find code that performs cryptography or dynamic execution.

low_findings.title: API Search Pattern Is Not Network Activity
low_findings.description: The network alert maps to a grep pattern for finding API calls in a target codebase. It is not an HTTP request made by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a bash search-pattern block and contains grep text for fetch, axios, and requests. No actual network client is imported or called by the skill script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Local File Reads
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper recursively walks the selected root and reads matching Python files to find imports, callers, and tests. This is expected behavior for the skill, but it can inspect sensitive local source if the root is too broad.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem traversal and read_text calls are explicit and central to the helper. The pattern is dangerous only when used outside trusted project scopes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Shell Search Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill gives shell command examples for grep, find, and xargs. These commands are useful for code search, but users should review scope before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documentation rather than executed code, but the workflow can lead an agent to run local shell searches. No command injection vector was found in the provided examples.

content.value_statement: Developers often miss prior decisions, import relationships, and tests before changing unfamiliar code. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to gather memory context and local code impact details first.
actual_capabilities: Detects coding situations where extra context is useful, such as debugging, refactoring, or architectural planning.
Guides memory graph searches for related entities, decisions, project context, and ownership clues.
Provides command patterns for finding imports, callers, implementations, tests, routes, and configuration references.
Includes a Python helper that analyzes imports, dependents, callers, and tests for Python targets.
Recommends concise summaries that combine memory context with code relationship findings.
limitations: The helper focuses on Python files and does not build full semantic call graphs for every language.
Memory graph quality depends on the available MCP memory data in the user environment.
Search examples may need adaptation for large monorepos, generated files, or nonstandard project layouts.
Broad searches can surface sensitive configuration details if used without scoping.

use_cases.title: Review an unfamiliar module
use_cases.description: Find the module purpose, imports, dependents, and tests before making the first change.

use_cases.title: Assess refactor impact
use_cases.description: Identify callers, importers, and related tests before renaming or moving shared code.

use_cases.title: Recover project rationale
use_cases.description: Search memory graph entries for prior decisions and connect them to current implementation work.

prompt_templates.title: Find basic file context
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check before editing one file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-context-finder to summarize the purpose, imports, dependents, and tests for this file before I edit it.

prompt_templates.title: Map a feature area
prompt_templates.scenario: Understand related code and memory graph context
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-context-finder for this feature. Search memory for related decisions, then identify relevant files, callers, routes, and tests.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a refactor safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare impact analysis for shared code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-context-finder to analyze the impact of changing this module. Include direct dependents, likely indirect impacts, tests, and prior design context.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate an architectural change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use code-context-finder to compare this proposed architecture with existing patterns. Include memory graph decisions, similar implementations, dependencies, risks, and test gaps.

output_examples.input: Find context for an unfamiliar authentication service before editing it.
output_examples.output: The response lists related memory entries, the service imports, files that import it, likely callers, and matching tests. It ends with focused review actions.

output_examples.input: Assess the impact of renaming a shared helper function.
output_examples.output: The response identifies direct callers, dependent modules, relevant test files, and previous design notes that could affect the rename plan.

output_examples.input: Investigate a failing checkout flow with unknown ownership.
output_examples.output: The response connects error terms to memory graph entries, highlights related components, and lists files and tests to inspect first.

best_practices: Scope searches to the project directory and avoid unrestricted roots.
Review memory graph context before changing architectural or shared code.
Summarize only the context needed for the current task to reduce noise.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad searches across home directories or unrelated repositories.
Do not paste secrets or full environment files into the AI conversation.
Do not treat grep results as a complete semantic dependency graph.

faq.question: Does this skill change code by itself?
faq.answer: No. It helps gather context before coding and can guide local relationship analysis.

faq.question: Which languages does the helper script support?
faq.answer: The included helper focuses on Python imports, callers, dependents, and tests.

faq.question: Can it use project memory?
faq.answer: Yes. It is designed to query MCP memory tools when they are available.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network requests in the reviewed skill files.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The workflow can inspect local source and configuration references, which may expose sensitive project context.

faq.question: How should teams use it safely?
faq.answer: Run it only in trusted project roots and avoid including secrets or unrelated directories in searches.
